disclaimer: The content is intended as general guidance for symptom recognition in home growing. Diagnoses are always probabilities. Multiple problems can occur at the same time.

problems.name: Yellow Leaves
problems.subtitle: Common: nitrogen deficiency (N deficiency)
problems.overview: A nitrogen deficiency usually shows up first on older, lower leaves. The plant reallocates mobile nitrogen into new growth, causing the lower leaves to become lighter, turn yellow, and later die off.
key_symptoms: Yellowing usually begins on lower, older leaves
The plant looks overall lighter or paler than usual
Yellow leaves wilt, can develop brown spots, and later fall off
Growth slows down
New shoots often stay greener than older foliage at first
likely_causes: Insufficient nutrient supply during vegetative growth
pH in the root zone is blocking uptake
Depleted substrate or going too long without feeding again
Root damage caused by waterlogging or lack of oxygen
Heavy flushing without a properly adjusted nutrient follow-up
how_to_confirm: Check whether the symptoms started at the bottom of the plant
Review the feeding schedule from the last 7 to 14 days
Inspect the root zone: a constantly wet medium can also contribute to deficiency symptoms
Compare whether the yellowing is mostly even rather than patchy
immediate_actions: Stabilize pH within the target range
Plan a moderate, growth-stage-appropriate nitrogen feeding instead of overcorrecting
Check watering habits and avoid waterlogging
Only remove heavily damaged, fully dead leaves once they no longer contribute

treatment_plan.title: Narrow down the cause
treatment_plan.details: Check pH and watering rhythm first, then the actual nutrient amount. A real nitrogen deficiency is often not only a fertilizer shortage, but sometimes an uptake lockout.

treatment_plan.title: Correct gently
treatment_plan.details: Increase nutrient supply carefully and adjust it to the current stage. Do not use aggressive overfeeding as a quick fix.

treatment_plan.title: Monitor progress
treatment_plan.details: New leaves are the best indicator. Already yellow leaves often do not turn green again; what matters is stabilizing the new growth.

treatment_plan.title: Avoid follow-up mistakes
treatment_plan.details: Do not water heavily, repot, and fertilize aggressively all at once. Too many corrections at the same time make diagnosis harder.

prevention: Use grow nutrients appropriate for the stage
Do not let the substrate become depleted, especially during longer vegetative periods
Check pH regularly
Maintain a consistent watering rhythm instead of constant wetness
Watch leaf color and growth speed early
common_confusions: Natural aging of individual lower leaves late in flowering
pH lockout instead of a true shortage
General nutrient deficiency after heavy flushing
red_flags: Yellowing quickly moves up through the whole plant
New growth also turns strongly yellow
A rotten smell or constantly wet medium is present as well
No improvement in new growth despite feeding
problems.when_to_escalate: If new growth keeps getting lighter after correcting pH and watering habits, or if several deficiency patterns appear at the same time, the overall strategy for medium, EC, and root health should be reviewed.

problems.name: Mold
problems.subtitle: Typical: powdery mildew or bud rot
problems.overview: Mold appears especially with high humidity, poor air movement, and damp microclimates. Common forms are white residue on leaves (powdery mildew) or flowers rotting from the inside (bud rot/Botrytis).
key_symptoms: White, powdery residue on leaves or leaf stems
Brown, gray, or mushy spots in buds
Flower parts die off from the inside
Musty or rotten smell
In dense plants, the infection often spreads quickly
likely_causes: Humidity that is too high
Weak circulation or stagnant air pockets
Dense canopy or very compact buds
Wet plant parts for extended periods
Spores introduced through plant material, clothing, or an unhygienic environment
how_to_confirm: Inspect white residue carefully: powdery mildew usually sits on the surface
Gently open buds and check for internal browning or gray fungal tissue
Watch for local humidity pockets in a dense canopy
Check whether the infection is concentrated in shaded, poorly ventilated zones
immediate_actions: Immediately isolate and cleanly remove affected plant parts
Lower humidity and improve air movement
Do not use heavily infected buds any further
Clean tools, hands, and work surfaces after contact

treatment_plan.title: Stop the spread
treatment_plan.details: Remove visibly affected areas immediately. With bud rot, it is better to cut generously than too narrowly.

treatment_plan.title: Correct the climate
treatment_plan.details: Lower moisture, improve air exchange, and reduce dead, dense plant material. During flowering, dry, well-moving air and no stagnant damp pockets are crucial.

treatment_plan.title: Monitor the remaining plants closely
treatment_plan.details: Check critical zones several times a day: main cola, inner canopy, lower growth, and shaded areas.

treatment_plan.title: Maintain hygiene after harvest
treatment_plan.details: Do not dry or store material in overly humid conditions. Dispose of infected material separately.

prevention: Control humidity consistently during flowering
Ensure sufficient air circulation and fresh air exchange
Do not let plants grow unnecessarily dense
Avoid wet buds, condensation, and splash water
Use clean tools and clothing, and quarantine new plants
common_confusions: Mistaking trichomes on buds for mold
Mistaking dust or residue on leaves for powdery mildew
Late natural leaf aging instead of fungal infection
red_flags: Mold directly inside buds or main colas
Multiple infection sites at the same time
Musty smell in the tent or drying room
Infection shortly before harvest or during drying
problems.when_to_escalate: If the issue keeps returning, the entire climate and hygiene setup has to be reviewed: dehumidification, airflow, plant density, sanitation, and the drying process.

subtypes.name: Powdery mildew
subtypes.marker: White, powdery residue on leaf surfaces

problems.name: Pests
problems.subtitle: Common: spider mites, fungus gnats, thrips, aphids
problems.overview: Pest problems often begin subtly and escalate when scouting is not done early. Depending on the species, you may see sucking damage, silvery patches, stippling, sticky residue, larvae in the medium, or webbing.
key_symptoms: Light speckling, silvery scrape marks, or deformed leaves
Small insects on leaf undersides or in the substrate
Fine webbing with heavier spider mite infestations
Sticky honeydew residue with aphids
Small flying gnats above constantly moist substrate
likely_causes: Introduced through new plants, clones, clothing, or tools
Leaf undersides and substrate surface are checked too rarely
A constantly wet medium favors fungus gnats
Warm, dry climate favors spider mites
Lack of quarantine or poor hygiene
how_to_confirm: Check leaf undersides with a magnifier
Use yellow or blue sticky traps for early detection
Inspect the substrate surface for larvae and flying fungus gnats
Compare the damage pattern with typical feeding or sucking damage
Identify the pest species as closely as possible before treatment
immediate_actions: Isolate the affected plant if possible
Remove heavily infested leaves or hotspots
Adjust growing conditions: no waterlogged soil, no extreme heat-dryness
Match the treatment to the actual pest and do not spray blindly

treatment_plan.title: Identify the species
treatment_plan.details: Spider mites, thrips, aphids, and fungus gnats need different measures. A wrong treatment costs time and allows the infestation to spread.

treatment_plan.title: Reduce infestation pressure
treatment_plan.details: Remove heavily affected plant parts, clean the environment, and reduce adult populations with traps or targeted measures.

treatment_plan.title: Think about larvae and future generations
treatment_plan.details: Only hitting visible adult insects is often not enough. Repeated checks and, if necessary, beneficial organisms are important.

treatment_plan.title: Use IPM instead of a one-time measure
treatment_plan.details: Combine prevention, scouting, hygiene, climate, and targeted countermeasures.

prevention: Keep new plants in strict quarantine
Keep the grow room, tools, and hands clean
Regularly inspect leaf undersides and the pot surface
Use sticky traps as an early warning system
Manage watering and climate so that neither constant wetness nor extreme dry heat develops
common_confusions: Nutrient deficiency instead of sucking damage
Dust particles instead of mites or eggs
Overwatering instead of the fungus gnat root cause
Light stress instead of thrips scrape marks
red_flags: Many flying fungus gnats every time you water
Rapid infestation increase within a few days
Several plants affected at the same time
problems.when_to_escalate: If the infestation keeps increasing despite hygiene, climate adjustment, and monitoring, a consistent IPM approach with a species-specific strategy should be used.

pest_profiles.name: Spider mites
symptoms: rapid spread in warm, dry climate
focus_actions: Do not let the climate become hot and extremely dry

pest_profiles.name: Fungus gnats
symptoms: small black fliers above the medium
larvae in the moist top layer
often found in constantly wet soil
focus_actions: Let the surface dry between waterings
Improve substrate moisture management and hygiene

pest_profiles.name: Aphids
focus_actions: Consider hygiene and a beneficial insect strategy

problems.name: Overwatering
problems.subtitle: Watered too often or too much
problems.overview: Overwatering usually means not only too much water per watering, but above all watering too often. The medium stays wet for too long, the roots get too little oxygen, and the plant shows drooping, often heavy leaves as well as slowed growth.
key_symptoms: Drooping leaves despite a wet medium
Leaves look heavy, swollen, or clawed downward
Slow growth and a dull-looking plant
Yellowish leaves or deficiency symptoms despite nutrients being present
The pot stays unusually heavy and wet for a long time
likely_causes: Watering too often without a dry-back phase
Pot volume is too large for a small plant
Poor drainage or compacted substrate
Too few air pockets in the medium
Watering by schedule instead of actual need
how_to_confirm: Compare pot weight before and after watering
Use a finger test or measure moisture in the root zone
Inspect roots if suspicion is strong: brown, slimy, or foul-smelling roots are warning signs
Differentiate it from underwatering: overwatered leaves often feel heavier rather than dry and limp
immediate_actions: Pause watering until the medium has dried back appropriately
Indirectly support airflow around the root zone
Do not add extra nutrients as a quick guess-based fix

treatment_plan.title: Stop the water stress
treatment_plan.details: Do not water again as long as the pot is still clearly heavy and the medium remains wet.

treatment_plan.title: Improve drainage
treatment_plan.details: Check drainage holes, saucers, substrate structure, and air porosity. If the problem is chronic, a more airy medium often helps.

treatment_plan.title: Protect the root zone
treatment_plan.details: Waterlogging increases the risk of root problems and secondary deficiency patterns. Solve the oxygen problem first, then think about nutrients.

treatment_plan.title: Establish a new watering rhythm
treatment_plan.details: Water based on need, not on a rigid calendar. Pot weight and plant signals are more reliable than fixed intervals.

prevention: Match pot size to plant size
Use a substrate with good drainage and air capacity
Only water when the plant truly needs it
Do not leave saucers permanently filled with water
After each run, refine the watering routine based on pot weight and dry-back time
common_confusions: Underwatering: both can cause drooping leaves
Nitrogen deficiency caused by secondary uptake problems
Root diseases that were also triggered by prolonged wetness
red_flags: A rotten smell coming from the pot
The medium stays wet for several days
The plant gets worse after every additional watering
problems.when_to_escalate: If the plant does not perk up despite a watering pause, or if the roots look unhealthy, the issue should be treated as a root-zone or root-rot risk.
